critique.summary: The European Space Agency (ESA) has re-established communication with a previously lost spacecraft from its Proba-3 mission, which simulates a solar eclipse to study the Sun's corona. After losing contact due to a loss of orientation and battery drainage, the spacecraft was found to have some solar power, allowing ESA to reconnect and begin recovery efforts.
mainPoints: The Proba-3 mission involves two spacecraft flying in formation to simulate a solar eclipse and study the Sun's corona.
One spacecraft lost orientation on February 14, causing its solar panels to face away from the Sun and its batteries to drain.
The spacecraft entered survival mode and was silent for about a month.
ESA re-established contact after detecting some sunlight hitting the solar panels, allowing the spacecraft to recharge.
ESA plans to switch instruments back on and assess any damage to the spacecraft.
The mission aims to provide extended observations of the solar corona, which is normally difficult to study due to the Sun's brightness.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims align with publicly available information about the ESA's Proba-3 mission and its objectives to study the solar corona using formation flying spacecraft. The described incident of losing and regaining contact with one of the spacecraft is plausible and consistent with known challenges in space missions. Statements from ESA officials and mission managers add credibility. However, the article cites only ESA sources and does not provide multiple perspectives or independent verification, which limits the depth of corroboration.
considerations: The article does not mention any independent expert opinions or third-party verification of the spacecraft's status.
There is no detailed technical explanation of the cause of the spacecraft's loss of orientation or the recovery process.
Potential impacts on the mission timeline or scientific goals due to the incident are not discussed.
The article does not address the broader context of similar spacecraft anomalies or how common such recovery events are in space missions.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the Proba-3 mission's innovative approach to creating an artificial solar eclipse using two spacecraft, with some users appreciating the mission's technical achievement and others critiquing terminology and website usability.

comments.author: tetris11
comments.text:
> Proba-3, works just like a real solar eclipse. One spacecraft, which is roughly circular when viewed from the front, orbits closer to the sun, and its job is to block the bright parts of the sun, acting as the moon would in a real eclipse. It casts a shadow on a second probe that has a camera capable of photographing the resulting artificial eclipse.

> Having two separate spacecraft flying independently but in such a way that one casts a shadow on the other is a challenging task. But future missions depend on scientists figuring out how to make this precision choreography technology work, and so Proba-3 is a test.

Oh wow, they've potentially rescued this (very cool!) mission for both probes
comments.analysis: This comment accurately summarizes the mission's technical details and expresses enthusiasm about the mission's recovery, reflecting a good understanding of the subject.

comments.author: graemep
comments.text: The European Space Agency, not 'Europe'. Just as annoying as calling the EU Europe, and calling both Europe despite different membership is just confusing.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly points out the common misnomer of conflating Europe with the European Space Agency, highlighting an important distinction that can cause confusion.

comments.author: touwer
comments.text: I'm glad that this site at least left ~10% of the screen on my phone to read the article, next to all banners and newsletter ads. It's not 5%, great!
comments.analysis: This comment expresses frustration with website design and ads but does not contribute meaningfully to the discussion about the mission or article content.
